😷 Tonsillitis in Children

Managing a sore throat with safe, parent-friendly advice.
Dr. Murali Gopal
Senior Paediatrician & Paediatric Pulmonologist
MCR: 57489
MBBS, DCH(UK), MRCPCH(UK), FRCPCH(UK), CCT Paediatrics (UK), Fellow in Paediatric Pulmonology (Aus), Allergology (Ind)
Last reviewed: 10 May 2026
Reassurance for parentsTonsillitis is common in children and often improves with rest, fluids, and pain relief. Many cases are viral, but some children need medical assessment for bacterial infection or complications.

What is Tonsillitis?

Tonsillitis is infection and inflammation of the tonsils, the tissue pads at the back of the throat. The tonsils may become red, swollen, and painful.

Symptoms & Signs

  • Sore throat
  • Pain or difficulty swallowing
  • Fever
  • Swollen glands in the neck
  • Bad breath, ear pain, or reduced appetite

Causes

  • Usually caused by a virus
  • Sometimes caused by bacteria such as group A streptococcus
  • Spreads through droplets and close contact

Home Management

  • Offer fluids often and give soft foods if tolerated.
  • Use age-appropriate pain or fever medicine as advised.
  • Encourage rest.
  • Hand hygiene helps reduce spread.
  • Do not force food, but keep encouraging fluids.

Red Flags / When to Seek Help

  • Difficulty breathing
  • Drooling or inability to swallow fluids
  • Severe dehydration or very little urine
  • Severe one-sided throat pain, muffled voice, or neck swelling
  • Very sleepy, confused, or rapidly worsening illness

Important Facts / Myth correction

Many episodes are viral, so antibiotics are not always needed.
Pain control helps children drink and rest more comfortably.
If antibiotics are prescribed, the full course should be completed.

Legal medical disclaimer

This parent guide is for education only and does not replace a medical consultation, diagnosis, or treatment plan. Advice should be interpreted in the context of your child's age, symptoms, and medical history. If your child has worsening symptoms, breathing difficulty, dehydration, unusual drowsiness, persistent high fever, seizures, or any emergency warning signs, seek urgent medical care immediately. All final guides should be reviewed by a qualified medical professional before clinical use.

References

  • Royal Children’s Hospital Melbourne. Kids Health Info: Tonsillitis. https://www.rch.org.au/kidsinfo/fact_sheets/Tonsillitis/
